question: A 55-year-old man presents with sudden-onset left-sided sensory loss and visual blurring. An urgent non-contrast head CT is obtained prior to consideration of intravenous alteplase. Based on the imaging, which of the following is the most appropriate next action?
ground_truth_reasoning:
First, I examine the provided non-contrast axial head CT. The parenchyma appears symmetric, there are no focal hyperdense collections within the brain or extra-axial spaces to suggest acute intracranial hemorrhage, and there is no obvious large hypodense infarct, loss of grey–white differentiation, sulcal effacement, or midline shift. The ventricles and cortical sulci are visible and there is no radiographic mass effect.

Next, I relate these imaging findings to the clinical question, which asks what to do next before considering intravenous alteplase for a patient with sudden focal neurological symptoms. On non-contrast CT the critical contraindication that would preclude thrombolysis is an acute intracranial haemorrhage. Because the CT shows no hemorrhage or other acute imaging contraindication, the study does not provide a reason to withhold thrombolysis on imaging grounds.

Then I consider how the answer options align with the image: Option B (withhold alteplase because of hemorrhage) is not supported because no haemorrhage is seen. Option D (obtain MRI before treatment) is unnecessary as a prerequisite when CT already excludes hemorrhage and would delay urgent therapy. Option C (administer aspirin and observe) would be chosen if thrombolysis were contraindicated or not indicated, but the CT does not show such a contraindication. Therefore, based on the CT appearance alone, proceeding with intravenous alteplase is the most appropriate next action.

In summary, the non-contrast CT demonstrates no acute intracranial hemorrhage or other imaging contraindication to thrombolysis, so the correct action based on this imaging is to proceed with intravenous alteplase.
caption: a Pre-thrombolysis non-contrast brain CT head scan demonstrating normal appearances, and in particular no evidence of haemorrhage. b After thrombolysis multiple foci of intracerebral haemorrhage with bilateral posterior hemispheric predominance are evident. c-d Macroscopic images of coronal brain sections showing multiple haemorrhages. There are recent intraparenchymal haemorrhages at the right insula and superior temporal gyrus (c) and the right occipital lobe (d). Extension to the subarachnoid space and the inferior (c) and posterior horns (d) is evident. Mild midline shift to the left is also present and the right uncus is partly necrotic (c)
sub_caption: a Pre-thrombolysis non-contrast brain CT head scan demonstrating normal appearances, and in particular no evidence of haemorrhage.
image_context: A 55-year-old man was admitted to hospital as a stroke call, presenting with sudden-onset left sided cheek, arm and hand sensory loss, visual blurring, and worsening headache. The National Institutes of Health Stroke Scale (NIHSS) score was 3. His past medical history was significant for multiple co-morbidities including coronary artery bypass graft (CABG) surgery, hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and hypercholesterolemia. An urgent head CT excluded intracranial haemorrhage (Fig. <xref rid="12883_2022_3029_Fig1_HTML" ref-type="fig">1</xref>A), and intravenous thrombolysis with alteplase was administered. Within 2 hours of thrombolysis, his features of visual disturbance and headache worsened, with increasing confusion. A repeat CT of the head showed multiple areas of intracerebral haemorrhage, mainly in the bilateral posterior regions (Fig. A), and intravenous thrombolysis with alteplase was administered. Within 2 hours of thrombolysis, his features of visual disturbance and headache worsened, with increasing confusion. A repeat CT of the head showed multiple areas of intracerebral haemorrhage, mainly in the bilateral posterior regions (Fig. <xref rid="12883_2022_3029_Fig1_HTML" ref-type="fig">1</xref>B). No benefit from neurosurgical intervention was expected. Despite intensive multiple organ support therapy, he deteriorated over his admission, developed chest infection, and died 4 days after the initial presentation. The case was referred to HM Coroner and detailed neuropathological examination was performed.B). No benefit from neurosurgical intervention was expected. Despite intensive multiple organ support therapy, he deteriorated over his admission, developed chest infection, and died 4 days after the initial presentation. The case was referred to HM Coroner and detailed neuropathological examination was performed.Fig. 1a Pre-thrombolysis non-contrast brain CT head scan demonstrating normal appearances, and in particular no evidence of haemorrhage. b After thrombolysis multiple foci of intracerebral haemorrhage with bilateral posterior hemispheric predominance are evident. c-d Macroscopic images of coronal brain sections showing multiple haemorrhages. There are recent intraparenchymal haemorrhages at the right insula and superior temporal gyrus (c) and the right occipital lobe (d). Extension to the subarachnoid space and the inferior (c) and posterior horns (d) is evident. Mild midline shift to the left is also present and the right uncus is partly necrotic (c) Coronal sections of the cerebral hemispheres revealed mild midline shift to the left associated with compressed ventricles in the anterior and central parts (Fig. <xref rid="12883_2022_3029_Fig1_HTML" ref-type="fig">1</xref>C). The right inferior horn and the posterior horns were filled with blood clots (Fig. C). The right inferior horn and the posterior horns were filled with blood clots (Fig. <xref rid="12883_2022_3029_Fig1_HTML" ref-type="fig">1</xref>C-D). There were multiple recent intracerebral parenchymal haemorrhages, various in size and widespread in distribution, affecting the right frontobasal area, the right temporo-occipital lobe, left superior parietal lobe, both posterior occipital lobe areas and the left cerebellar hemisphere. These haemorrhages probably occurred at the same time, and the atypical distribution suggested that they were secondary to the thrombolysis. Furthermore, a soft, cavitating area was identified in the subcortical white matter of the superior temporal gyrus and the insula slightly extending to the putamen, measuring 20x10x10mm. Timing was uncertain but appeared to be older than the haemorrhages, probably representing an ischaemic infarct. The hippocampi, the inferior part of the thalamus and the midbrain were haemorrhagic, soft and fragmented. Typical Duret’s haemorrhages were noted in the central part of the pons. The fourth ventricle contained a moderate amount of clotted blood. Summarizing the macroscopical findings, the direct cause of the death was given as brainstem herniation secondary to widespread recent intraparenchymal haemorrhages, probably related to thrombolysis. Alternative causes of the haemorrhages were also considered and further histological examination was performed.C-D). There were multiple recent intracerebral parenchymal haemorrhages, various in size and widespread in distribution, affecting the right frontobasal area, the right temporo-occipital lobe, left superior parietal lobe, both posterior occipital lobe areas and the left cerebellar hemisphere. These haemorrhages probably occurred at the same time, and the atypical distribution suggested that they were secondary to the thrombolysis. Furthermore, a soft, cavitating area was identified in the subcortical white matter of the superior temporal gyrus and the insula slightly extending to the putamen, measuring 20x10x10mm. Timing was uncertain but appeared to be older than the haemorrhages, probably representing an ischaemic infarct. The hippocampi, the inferior part of the thalamus and the midbrain were haemorrhagic, soft and fragmented. Typical Duret’s haemorrhages were noted in the central part of the pons. The fourth ventricle contained a moderate amount of clotted blood. Summarizing the macroscopical findings, the direct cause of the death was given as brainstem herniation secondary to widespread recent intraparenchymal haemorrhages, probably related to thrombolysis. Alternative causes of the haemorrhages were also considered and further histological examination was performed.

The National Institutes of Health Stroke Scale (NIHSS) score was 3. His past medical history was significant for multiple co-morbidities including coronary artery bypass graft (CABG) surgery, hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and hypercholesterolemia.
